Not applicable for this control: VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control 13.11

> Tune Security Event Alerting Thresholds

**SCF Controls:** MON-01.13

**Aggregate Coverage:** Meets

#### VCF (Meets)

VCF provides multiple capabilities for tuning event monitoring technologies, analyzing communication traffic and event patterns, and developing profiles that represent normal operational behavior.

VCF Operations includes both dynamic and static threshold mechanisms for alert generation. Dynamic thresholding is enabled by default and determines threshold values automatically based on analysis of historical data. An alert is generated when observed metrics violate the learned threshold. This capability directly addresses the control's requirement to develop profiles representing common patterns, as the system continuously analyzes historical metrics to establish baseline behavior. Static thresholds are also available, where administrators configure specific metric values and alerts fire when observations exceed or fall below those values. Both threshold types can be added and adjusted through VCF Operations threshold configuration.

Alert definition tuning is a supported workflow in VCF Operations. Administrators can create and refine alert definitions following documented best practices to optimize alert behavior for monitored objects. Alert definitions follow a symptom-based model where administrators define incremental levels of concern. Threshold sensitivity is configurable at the symptom level, and wait cycles can be set to control how quickly alerts fire after symptom conditions are met. Alert definition thresholds should be configured without gaps to maintain continuous alert coverage across the full range of monitored values. For example, administrators should ensure that threshold ranges for related alerts (such as a warning at 50% and a critical at 75%) do not leave gaps that could cause lower-severity alerts to cancel without triggering higher-severity ones. Minimizing unnecessary alerts in VCF Operations is approached by adjusting alert generation parameters rather than suppressing alerts after the fact.

VCF Operations Intelligent Alerts further refine monitoring output by automating alert correlation and filtering. This reduces alert flooding and helps administrators focus on the most significant issues rather than processing large volumes of duplicative alerts from multiple monitoring tools detecting the same problem.

For network traffic analysis and pattern development, VCF Operations Network Operations provides entity metrics through NetFlow Reporter for monitoring network traffic and transmission errors. Flow-based application discovery analyzes traffic patterns and network flows to identify applications in the environment, directly building the traffic profiles this control requires. NSX Live Traffic Analysis monitors traffic at a source or between source and destination, with packet capture to identify packet drops and bad flows between endpoints. VCF Operations for Networks supports search-based alerts that can be configured to detect specific traffic patterns or anomalies, with configurable severity levels of Critical, Moderate, Warning, and Info. The Recommended Firewall Rules API retrieves recommended rules based on flow traffic observed between groups, reflecting analysis of actual communication patterns in the environment. VCF Operations also displays the top firewall rules based on flow traffic, providing visibility into which rules are handling the most network activity.

VCF Operations supports event trend and anomaly analysis through its log analysis capabilities. Administrators can construct queries and view results in the Event Trends tab to analyze log events for trends and anomalies over time. User-defined events from VCF Operations for Networks can be collected as notifications, and VCF Operations can be configured to collect events, tasks, and alarms from VMware vCenter as well as logs from VCF Operations for Networks and VCF Log Management, providing a broad event data set for pattern analysis.

The vCenter alarm infrastructure integrates with events and performance counters, enabling administrators to create alarm definitions with automated responses to threshold violations, security events, and system state changes. VCF PowerCLI supports creating metric-based alarm triggers with the New-AlarmTrigger parameter to define thresholds and conditions for CPU usage and other performance metrics. vCenter can also create alarms to monitor events on vSAN objects, including the cluster, VMware ESX hosts, datastores, networks, and virtual machines. vCenter also includes an event burst filter that monitors the incoming event stream for identical events occurring over a short period and consolidates repeated occurrences into a single event before storage, reducing noise from event flooding in the event log.

VCF Log Management includes pre-built content packs with dashboards and alerts for VCF components, including NSX environment monitoring. These content packs provide pre-built dashboards, alerts, and queries that administrators can customize to match their environment's specific event patterns and operational norms.

For workloads deployed through VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) on the VCF platform, Prometheus with Alertmanager is available as a standard package installed in the tanzu-system-monitoring namespace. Prometheus collects time-series metrics from cluster components and forwards them to Alertmanager, which handles alert routing, grouping, inhibition, and notification based on configurable rules. Alerting rules are defined via the prometheus.config.alerting_rules_yml parameter, and alert routing behavior is governed through the AlertmanagerConfig custom resource, which specifies how alerts are grouped, inhibited, and dispatched to external notification targets with configurable group intervals and wait times. Alertmanager is deployed as a StatefulSet with 3 replicas for high availability in the tanzu-system-monitoring namespace. The kube-state-metrics component generates metrics on the state of Kubernetes objects, including pod counts, deployment readiness, and resource saturation, which can drive alerting rules when integrated with Prometheus. Individual cluster components expose instrumentation that supports threshold analysis: the kube-apiserver publishes apiserver_watch_events_total to track watch event volume by group, resource, and version; the kubelet exposes pod lifecycle event metrics including kubelet_evented_pleg_connection_success_count; and the Kubernetes scheduler exposes scheduler_inflight_events to monitor in-flight scheduling activity. API Priority and Fairness metrics expose data about whether API server configuration is throttling important traffic or processing poorly-behaved workloads, supporting threshold adjustments for API-level load patterns. Kubernetes Event objects record cluster state changes with microsecond-level timestamps and include a series field that captures repeated event occurrences, enabling identification of patterns that warrant alerting rules. The kubectl events command supports real-time event streaming via the --watch flag and filtering by event type via the --types flag. Istio, available as a standard package on VKS clusters, automatically generates metrics, logs, and distributed traces for all service mesh traffic including ingress and egress, providing per-service traffic data that forms the basis for network-level threshold configuration and normal traffic profile development. Native Kubernetes system component traces record the latency of and relationships between operations in the cluster, covering control-plane components such as the API server, kubelet, and scheduler, supplementing Prometheus metrics as a data source for establishing baseline cluster operation patterns and informing control-plane-level threshold configuration. Dynatrace, when deployed on VKS clusters via its operator, automatically instruments applications and infrastructure upon deployment, collecting metrics, logs, and traces to support monitoring profile development.

VCF Automation alert threshold values are set within VCF Operations, centralizing threshold management across the platform.

#### VMware vDefend (Meets)

VMware vDefend provides multiple mechanisms for tuning event monitoring technologies through traffic and event pattern analysis, spanning IDS/IPS signature management, Network Detection and Response (NDR) detector thresholds, and Network Traffic Analysis (NTA) for suspicious traffic.

vDefend Distributed IDS/IPS includes configurable threshold and rate-limiting controls that let administrators tune how and when alerts fire. Threshold settings define the minimum number of rule matches required before an alert is generated: the IDS/IPS generates an alert only after a rule matches the configured count within a defined time window. Limit thresholds restrict alert volume by generating at most one alert per host within a configurable time period. A combined "Both" threshold type applies thresholding and limiting together, generating a maximum of one alert if a signature triggers a specified number of times within a time window. Custom IDS/IPS signature rules include a `threshold` keyword that controls alert frequency directly within the rule syntax, providing per-rule tuning capability when deploying custom detection content. These controls allow administrators to tune IDS/IPS alerting to match their environment's traffic patterns and reduce false positives without losing detection coverage.

Security Services Platform NTA detectors include per-detector threshold values and exclusion lists managed from the Detector Activation tab. Detections falling below the configured threshold are discarded, allowing administrators to tune detection sensitivity based on their environment's baseline traffic patterns. NTA continuously monitors and analyzes network traffic and flow records using machine learning models and statistical techniques to identify deviations such as protocol misuse and unusual traffic patterns. Each NTA detector maps to a specific MITRE ATT&CK category or technique, providing structured context for tuning decisions.

NDR aggregates detections based on event type, activity type, and detection method, including matching the same IDS signature, helping administrators recognize patterns across multiple detections. Detection events correlate to form campaigns in NDR, allowing administrators to evaluate detection patterns at a higher level rather than responding to individual alerts. The unified event list can be filtered by detection impact score using checkboxes, and by MITRE ATT&CK tactic through histogram visualization, allowing administrators to focus on high-impact events and tune detection rules based on observed severity and tactic distributions. NDR Sensor alarms clear automatically when the triggering metric drops below the threshold. Security Services Platform alarms are categorized by severity (Critical, High, Medium, Informational), providing structured prioritization for alert management.

The Threat Event Monitoring dashboard in vDefend provides visibility into the current state of security issues in the data center, helping security teams understand network activity and identify areas that may require threshold adjustment. NDR integrates with external SIEM platforms including Splunk and VCF Log Management, and supports custom endpoints with configurable headers. NDR sends event logs to the configured SIEM when a detection event occurs, when an existing detection event is updated, or when a new campaign is created, providing real-time visibility into detection activity across the network. This integration allows organizations to incorporate vDefend detection events into broader monitoring workflows where additional tuning and correlation can occur.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Contributes)

VCF Protection and Recovery (PNR) provides configurable alarm thresholds, event monitoring tools, and integration channels that support alert threshold tuning within the recovery and replication domain.

PNR monitors connections between sites by sending periodic ping requests, logging connection check events to track connectivity health. Resource threshold alarms monitor consumption on the PNR Server host and raise alarms only when configurable resource thresholds are reached, allowing administrators to calibrate alert sensitivity to their operational baseline. PNR alarms can be configured to send email notifications, SNMP traps, or run custom scripts on the VMware vCenter host in response to events, giving administrators flexibility in how threshold violations are communicated. In multi-vCenter environments, PNR displays events from all PNR server instances registered as extensions, providing a unified view for cross-site monitoring.

vSphere Replication supports definition and editing of alarms to alert administrators when specific replication events occur, such as after configuring a virtual machine for replication. Alarms can be tuned to specific event types, and the event log provides a record for identifying patterns. PNR allows users to silence alerts on health findings when warnings or failures are not actionable, reducing noise in active monitoring environments.

The Clean Room Orchestrator adds a dedicated monitoring layer for isolated recovery environments. It maintains a running list of system-generated events and user actions that can be filtered by event category, event severity, or timestamp for monitoring and audit purposes. Event records include timestamp, priority level, message content, component name, severity level, configuration ID, component ID, and event type. The Clean Room Orchestrator sends email alerts when the status of the orchestrator or connectors changes, such as when status moves from good to warning. It also supports event forwarding to VCF Log Management, Microsoft Sentinel, or Splunk Cloud, enabling integration with enterprise SIEM platforms and centralized alerting systems.

VCF Operations extends PNR monitoring into a centralized operations console, providing an advanced alert system that notifies administrators when specific conditions are detected on protection groups, recovery plans, cloud file systems, recovery SDDCs, and protected sites.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer provides mechanisms for tuning event monitoring at the application delivery layer through configurable alert thresholds, severity levels, alert filter sensitivity, and notification throttle controls.

Administrators can create metric-based alert configurations from the Avi Controller, with each alert rule defining threshold values and multiple event conditions combined using logical operators to determine when notifications fire. Alert severity is classified as High, Medium, or Low. This classification governs how alerts are displayed in the Controller UI and how they are forwarded to remote systems.

For resource utilization monitoring, Avi supports dual-threshold tuning through the watermark_thresholds parameter in the Service Engine group analytics policy, which defines the threshold level for triggering metric events and alerts, and a corresponding reset threshold that clears the alert once utilization drops below it. These thresholds are adjustable to match the traffic and load patterns observed in a given deployment. The metric-based alert configuration also includes a throttle parameter that sets the minimum time interval between consecutive alert notifications, giving administrators control over notification frequency without sacrificing visibility into persistent conditions.

Alert filter sensitivity can be adjusted to make the system more or less responsive to generating new alerts, allowing operators to reduce noise from routine traffic variations or increase sensitivity during incident response periods.

Alert notifications can be delivered through multiple channels: the local Controller UI, email, syslog, SNMP v2c or SNMPv3 traps, and ControlScript, all configured in the Operations > Notifications page. The Avi Controller supports multiple SNMP servers for alert delivery and can configure SNMP notifications with TLS encryption and strict certificate verification. Custom alert actions can be created to supplement or replace the default system alert actions such as System-Alert-Level-High, allowing organizations to build notification and response workflows tailored to specific traffic patterns and event conditions.

The Service Engine Analytics Anomalies Overlay displays periods of abnormal traffic detection based on moving averages, generating anomaly detection events with timestamps when each anomaly is detected. Granularity is adjustable by changing the time interval. The Config Events Overlay correlates configuration changes such as adding, deleting, or modifying pools, virtual services, or Service Engines with traffic metrics, enabling administrators to identify whether traffic anomalies were caused by configuration modifications and supporting an iterative tuning process.

Not applicable for this control: VMware Data Services Manager. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of this product.

### Control 15.4

> Ensure Service Provider Contracts Include Security Requirements

**SCF Controls:** IAO-03.2, PRI-07.1, TPM-03.2, TPM-04, TPM-05

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF provides multiple layers of access control, identity federation, tenant isolation, and monitoring to mitigate risks associated with third-party access to infrastructure assets, applications, services, and data.

VCF SSO integrates with federated identity providers through standards-based protocols, supporting SAML 2.0, OIDC, and AD/LDAP as authentication sources. LDAP-based directory services such as Active Directory are supported for identity management across the platform, including NSX Manager, which supports both Active Directory over LDAP and Open LDAP as identity sources. This allows organizations to extend their existing identity governance to VCF rather than maintaining separate credential stores for third-party users.

Role-based access control enforces least-privilege principles across the platform. VCF local accounts support configurable roles such as superAdmin, and VCF Automation assigns host switch-level permissions through the Cloud Administrator group (Cloudadmin role). The administrators role in VCF Operations is restricted to specific users who need access to objects and actions across the entire environment. For Kubernetes workloads, vSphere Supervisor namespaces support role-based access control with EDIT, VIEW, and OWNER roles that grant specific privileges to users or groups. The Namespace Owner/Edit/View role scopes consumers of a vSphere Namespace to provisioning VKS clusters and vSphere Pods using kubectl, while vSphere Administrator and Supervisor Administrator roles have broader access to cluster and namespace resources. This layered role model restricts third-party access to specific workload boundaries.

VCF Automation implements a provider-and-consumer model that separates the operators of services from the organizations that consume them. Partner services from external vendors are integrated through this model, where providers manage services and organizations consume the resulting business outcomes such as self-service provisioning of Kubernetes clusters, databases, and other resources. Service providers control the publication scope of service instances to selected organizations and decide which roles, rights bundles, and UI plug-ins to include when publishing services. Provider Consumption Organizations offer additional administrative capabilities on top of standard organizations, allowing provider administrators to provision workloads while maintaining separation from consumer tenants. This architectural separation limits third-party visibility and access to only the resources they need to operate.

For third-party application integration, VCF Automation supports dedicated service accounts that automate access for third-party applications. Organizations can generate and issue API access tokens that applications use on their behalf, providing a controlled mechanism for programmatic access rather than requiring shared user credentials. The VCF Operations orchestrator presents an open architecture for plugging in external third-party applications, with integration points managed through the platform rather than through direct infrastructure access.

Tenant isolation is enforced through VCF Automation Organizations, which provide secure infrastructure isolation, quotas, and chargeback across multiple tenants. Within each organization, further isolation exists at the project and namespace levels. IT teams and cloud service providers can monitor resource usage, control permissions, and provide managed services through these organizations while network isolation at the NSX segment level restricts lateral movement between tenants. An Enterprise Admin can share resources with projects in a controlled manner, making objects available for consumption only within designated boundaries. App isolation policies in VCF Automation allow deployments that require on-demand security groups to enforce vDefend firewall rules at the application level. Data-in-transit communications default to TLS encryption for platform services.

VCF Operations monitors resource usage and policy compliance across the environment, providing visibility into third-party activity. VCF Operations supports firewall hardening that restricts network access to internal services, limiting the network paths available to third-party integrations. Service Discovery identifies permanent listening TCP ports and established UDP connections, and service discovery dashboards provide ongoing visibility into the services operating in the environment. VCF also provides capabilities to discover objects that are not compliant with a security standard, supporting ongoing risk identification for configurations affected by third-party access.

VMware Private AI Services (PAIS) provides technical mechanisms that contribute to mitigating risks associated with third-party access to AI-related assets, services, and data. These mechanisms operate at the trust validation, authentication, authorization, and content approval layers.

Trust Establishment for External Integrations

PAIS uses a CA trust bundle to establish secure HTTPS communication with external services and databases. Administrators must import CA trust bundles for the OIDC provider, Private Harbor registry, content management systems used as data sources in Data Indexing and Retrieval, MCP servers providing tools for agents, and the database server when those entities are not supplied at activation. Without an explicitly configured CA trust bundle, PAIS will not establish connections to those services, so third-party integrations require deliberate administrative action rather than automatic open access. PAIS supports updating certificate content for trusted entities through the same trust bundle mechanism, allowing administrators to refresh trust material as provider certificates change.

Access Control for Services and Data

PAIS access is controlled through OIDC group-based authorization. User accounts must be members of specified authorized groups (configured via the authorizedGroups setting) to access the service, and the oidc-groups-claim setting controls which OIDC claim is consulted for group identification in the organization namespace. PAIS requires HTTPS-based communication for OIDC provider integration. SharePoint data sources used as Knowledge Base inputs require user name and password authentication, with support limited to SharePoint On-Premises deployments, so the credential and connectivity posture for that integration is explicit rather than implicit.

Harbor Access and Model Storage

The Model Gallery uses Harbor project access capabilities to restrict access to the sensitive data used to train and tune models. PAIS integrates with Harbor registries as a model storage backend, requiring authentication and certificate-based trust for model retrieval. These controls help administrators scope which users and workloads can push to or pull from a registry used by PAIS.

Model Validation Before Deployment

Before uploading a model to a PAIS Model Gallery, the platform recommends evaluating the model's performance and safety for the intended business use case, including examining inference requests for malicious behavior and performing hands-on functional tests. PAIS guidance directs administrators to distribute models within the organization that have been validated on a Deep Learning VM rather than models pulled from the Internet, which could contain malicious code or be tuned for malicious behavior. This workflow helps administrators verify third-party or externally sourced models before they become available for production use.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend contributes to mitigating risks from third-party access to organizational technology assets, applications, services, and data through network-level access controls, threat detection, and security posture reporting. Identity governance, authentication, and administrative access controls are handled by other VCF components (VMware vCenter, VCF SSO). vDefend enforces the network security boundary that constrains what third parties can reach once they have been granted access.

vDefend enforces microsegmentation policies at the vNIC level, allowing administrators to define granular network access rules that restrict third-party workloads or connections to only the specific application tiers and services they require. Context-aware rules allow policies to incorporate workload-level attributes, limiting the network reach of third-party connections to defined sets of addresses, ports, and protocols. In multi-tenant VCF environments, the VPC Isolation with Essential Services strategy allows enterprise administrators to enforce strict inter-project isolation at the Distributed Firewall level while delegating more granular control to application owners within each project. This architecture is suited to environments where third-party development teams or external service providers operate within shared infrastructure, because Distributed Firewall policy at the project boundary can be set independently of what individual tenants configure inside their own VPCs.

vDefend provides north-south traffic filtering at the perimeter of logical network segments. Enterprise administrators can enforce isolation between projects by denying inter-project communication except for specified infrastructure protocols, adding an enforcement point for controlling inbound and outbound access from external or third-party networks. This policy layer applies regardless of what routing exists at the NSX level, giving security teams a dedicated control plane for third-party network boundaries.

vDefend IDS/IPS monitors east-west and north-south traffic for malicious activity, detecting exploitation attempts that may arise from compromised third-party connections. Signature rules support storing TLS and SSL certificates on disk for forensic analysis and threat investigation. Custom signature sources, including those derived from cybersecurity advisories, can be deployed to address threats specific to proprietary or custom applications that third parties introduce into the environment.

Security Services Platform Security Segmentation capabilities identify risky application protocols, including HTTP, Telnet, FTP, TFTP, LDAP, POP3, and IMAP, in use across the environment. This flags workloads, including those operated by third parties, that transport data without encryption, and allows administrators to restrict or block them as part of the security baseline. The Security Segmentation Report identifies at-risk workloads across categories including Public Traffic, Obsolete OS, Risky Application Protocols, and Inter VDI Server Connections, providing a structured view of workload-level risk that is directly relevant to understanding the exposure introduced by third-party access. Application monitoring in Security Services Platform surfaces security risks identified across workloads, allowing administrators to take targeted remediation steps against risky or non-compliant third-party workloads.

Security Services Platform certificate management supports importing CA bundles and web proxy certificates to establish trust for external service connections, including those used by the Malware Prevention Service. This controls which external services the platform can reach and provides a configuration point for managing trust relationships with third-party service endpoints.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer (Avi) provides application-layer mechanisms relevant to protecting sensitive and regulated data in transit at the application delivery tier.

For data in transit, each virtual service that handles encrypted connections is configured with an SSL/TLS profile that specifies the accepted cipher suites and protocol versions. These profiles are configurable per virtual service, allowing organizations to enforce strong cryptographic standards on connections carrying sensitive or regulated data. The System-Secure-HTTP application profile provides an additional layer of protection by enforcing HTTPS, protecting cookies, managing client IP handling, and controlling protocol behavior for HTTP virtual services.

TLS session persistence state is stored in encrypted form at the Service Engine so that session data is not exposed when a client reconnects to a different Service Engine. Communication between Avi Controllers and Service Engines uses encrypted channels.

Avi's Analytics Profile includes a sensitive log profile capability that controls how sensitive data appearing in client requests is handled in application logs. Operators configure this setting to suppress or mask fields that may contain regulated information, reducing the risk of sensitive data being written to log storage in cleartext.

In Kubernetes environments, the Avi Kubernetes Operator (AKO) supports PKIProfile resources that enable certificate-based authentication and encryption for communication with backend services. The RouteBackendExtension custom resource definition provides backend TLS configuration capability, supporting encryption of traffic between Service Engines and backend servers. The System-Standard SSL Profile can be applied through AKO to enable SSL/TLS re-encryption for backend traffic using modern cipher suites.

The Avi Cloud Console Application Rules service allows organizations to protect applications from known vulnerabilities by enabling managed application security rules on their Avi Controllers.

Fully satisfying this control also requires an organizational data classification program that identifies which data is sensitive or regulated under applicable contracts, maps that data to the application flows handled by Avi virtual services, and verifies that Avi's encryption and logging controls are applied to those flows. Avi provides the technical mechanisms; the organizational program determines how and where they are applied.
